elements.sent-2411-001: We consider a generalized derivative nonlinear Schr\"odinger equation.
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove existence of wave operator under an explicit smallness of the given asymptotic states.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our method bases on studying the associated system used in \cite{Tinpaper4 .}
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we show that if the initial data is small enough in $H^2(\R)$ then the associated solution scatters up to a Gauge transformation in sense of Theorem \ref{thm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-005: We consider the following equation \begin{equation}\label{eq1} iu_t+u_{xx}+i|u|^{2\sigma}u_x=0,\end{equation} for given $\sigma>0$, $u:\R_t\times\R_x\rightarrow\C$ is unknown function and $u_x$ denotes the derivative in space of the function $u$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Local well posedness of \eqref{eq1 was studied in \cite{HaOz16 on general domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: More precisely, for $\sigma\geq 1$, the authors proved that \eqref{eq1 is local well posedness in $H^1$ and global in time if initial data is small enough.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, in the case $0<\sigma<1$, there exists a solution in $(C_{w}\cap L^{\infty )(\R,H^1)$ for given initial data in $H^1$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the special case $\sigma=1$, \eqref{eq1 is known to be completely integrable.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Global well posedness was studied in \cite{Wu15 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The author proved that for each initial data in $H^1(\R)$ with the mass bounded by $4\pi$, the associated solution is global and uniformly bounded in $H^1(\R)$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Later, in \cite{FuHaIn17 , the authors improved this result by using variational argument.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Moreover, the authors showed that the solutions of \eqref{eq1 is global if its mass equals $4\pi$ and its momentum is negative.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Using integrability of \eqref{eq1 , solutions of \eqref{eq1 were proved global in $H^{2,2 (\R)$ by \cite{JeLiPeSu18 and in a subset of $H^2(\R)\cap H^{1,1 (\R)$ by \cite{PeSh18 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: To our knowledge, the best result is given in \cite{BaPe22 showing that solutions to \eqref{eq1 are global in $H^{\frac{1 {2 (\R)$ and its $H^{\frac{1 {2 (\R)$ norm remains globally bounded in time.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The equation \eqref{eq1 admits two parameters family of solitary waves solutions (or solitons) $u_{\omega,c}$ for $c\in (-2\sqrt{\omega ,2\sqrt{\omega )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Stability and instability of such solutions were studied in many works (see for instance \cite{MiTaXu23 , \cite{Gu18 , \cite{LiSiSu13 , \cite{GuNiWu20 , \cite{Fu17 , \cite{CoOh06 , \cite{Hayashi22 , \cite{KwWu18 , \cite{NiOhWu17 , \cite{Ning20 , \cite{Kim24 and references therein).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: It turns out that stability and instability of solitons depend on parameters $\sigma,\omega,c$.
elements.sent-2411-019: More precisely, solitons are unstable if $\sigma\geq 2$, stable if $0<\sigma\leq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-020: When $\sigma\in (1,2)$, there exists $z_0\in (-1,1)$ such that solitons are stable if $-2\sqrt{\omega <c<2z_0\sqrt{\omega}$ and unstable if $2z_0\sqrt{\omega}\leq c<2\sqrt{\omega}$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: There are special solutions, called multi-solitons, of \eqref{eq1 which behave in large time like sum of finite solitons \cite{Tinpaper3 , \cite{Tinpaper4 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Stability of such solutions was studied in \cite{CoWu18 when $\sigma=1$ and \cite{TaXu18 when $\sigma\in (1,2)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: More precisely, the authors proved that sum of finite numbers of stable solitons is stable in some sense.
elements.sent-2411-024: Scattering theory of \eqref{eq1 was first studied in \cite{BaWuXu20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The authors showed that when $\sigma\geq 2$, solutions are global and scattering when the initial data is small in $H^s$ for $\frac{1 {2}\leq s\leq 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, when $0<\sigma<2$, there exists a class of solitons which are close arbitrary zero, this is against the small data scattering statement.
elements.sent-2411-027: Relating to scattering theory, in \cite{Oz96,HaOz1994 , the authors proved existence of modified wave operator when $\sigma=1$ under a small condition on the asymptotic state given at infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, the modified scattering operator for derivative nonlinear Schr\"odinger equation was proved in \cite{GuHaLiNa13 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Recently, in \cite{baishen23 , the authors proved existence of wave operator by given asymptotic state in $H^1$ for all $\sigma\in\N$, $\sigma\geq 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The authors used a Gauge transformation and the normal form method to deal with the appearance of the derivative term in the nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we prove existence of wave operator under an explicit smallness of given asymptotic state by another approach, specially, we remove the assumption $\sigma\in\N$ used in \cite{baishen23 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The equation \eqref{eq1 admits three conserved quantities: \begin{equation}(Energy) \quad E(u)=\frac{1}{2}\norm{u_x}^2_{L^2}-\frac{1}{2\sigma+2}\Re\int_{\R} i|u|^{2\sigma}\overline{u}u_x dx, (Mass) \quad M(u)=\norm{u}^2_{L^2}, (Momentum) \quad P(u)=\Re\int_{\R}iu_x\overline{u}dx.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-095: A pair $(q,r)$ is called an admissible in dimension one if $q,r>0$ and $\frac{2 {q +\frac{1 {r =\frac{1 {2}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We define $\mathcal{A}$ to be the set of all admissible pairs.
elements.sent-2411-097: For a function $f(z)$ defined for a complex variable $z$ and for a positive integer $k$, we define $k$th order complex derivative of $f(z)$ by: \begin{equation}f^{k}(z):=\left(\frac{\partial^k f}{\partial_z^k}, \frac{\partial^k}{\partial_z^{k-1}\partial_{\overline{z}}}, \cdots, \frac{\partial^k f}{\partial_z\partial_{\overline{z}}^{k-1}},\frac{\partial^k f}{\partial_{\overline{z}}^k}\right),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\frac{\partial f}{\partial_z}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}-i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right), \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial_{\overline{z}}}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial f}{\partial_z}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}-i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right), \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial_{\overline{z}}}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}+i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right).\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-105: As in \cite{Tinpaper4 , we see that if $u$ shows \eqref{eq1 then $(\varphi,\psi)$ show the following system: \begin{equation}\label{eqsystem} \begin{cases} L\varphi =P(\varphi,\psi):=i\sigma |\varphi|^{2(\sigma-1)}\varphi^2\overline{\psi}-\sigma(\sigma-1)\varphi\int_{-\infty}^x|\varphi|^{2(\sigma-2)}\Im(\psi^2\overline{\varphi}^2), L\psi =Q(\varphi,\psi):=-i\sigma |\varphi|^{2(\sigma-1)}\psi^2\overline{\varphi}-\sigma(\sigma-1)\psi\int_{-\infty}^x|\varphi|^{2(\sigma-2)}\Im(\psi^2\overline{\varphi}^2). \end{cases}\end{equation} Let $\eta=(\varphi,\psi)$ and $F=(P,Q)$.}}
